results.title: Paper Synopsis | Reinforcement Learning for Long-Horizon Interactive LLM Agents (LOOP)
results.content:
{

 "action": "create_event",

 "calendar": "default",

 "title": "Meeting with Alex",

 "start_time": "2025-05-02T15:00:00",

 "attendees": ["alex@example.com"]

}
This sequence of output tokens is one rollout. If the calendar service responds successfully, confirming the event was scheduled correctly, the agent gets a reward of 1.0. An alternate rollout might generate:

{

 "action": "create_event",

 "calendar": "default",

 "title": "Friday Meeting",

 "start_time": "2025-05-09T15:00:00", // wrong date (next Friday)

 "attendees": []

}
This rollout misunderstood the date and didn’t include Alex as an attendee, resulting in a reward of 0.3. Assume we have three rollouts:

Per Token Importance Weighting:This method yielded the best results by assigning weights to each individual token the agent generates, rather than evaluating entire turns or trajectories. By examining the agent’s thought process under a microscope, it can fine-tune its policy and make more informed decisions in the future. It’s not just about what the agent does, but also how it arrives at its decisions.

The authors of “Reinforcement Learning for Long-Horizon Interactive LLM Agents” have not released an official codebase. Key innovations like leave-one-out baselines and per-token clipping can be integrated into existing RL pipelines to approximate LOOP’s performance.

It admits only tasks with “verifiable outcomes” and standardizes grading around structured deliverable- and milestone-based checks, avoiding an LLM judge wherever a deterministic alternative exists. That is a deliberate move all the way toward the verifiable end of the axis.

results.title: Don't Lose the Thread: Empowering Long-Horizon LLM Agents with...
results.content: Abstract: Agents powered by large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable progress in solving complex reasoning tasks. However, LLM agents often falter on long-horizon tasks due to cognitive overload, as their working memory becomes cluttered with expanding and irrelevant information, which dilutes their attention and hinders effective planning and reasoning. To mitigate this challenge, we introduce \\CO\\gnitive \\R\\esource Self-\\AL\\location (\\CORAL\\), a novel reasoning paradigm that empowers agents to proactively optimize their context. Implemented as an agent-callable working memory management toolset, CORAL allows an agent to maintain crucial checkpoints of its progress within its working memory and adaptively initiate a new problem-solving episode by purging [...] initiate a new problem-solving episode by purging cluttered working memory and resuming its reasoning from the most recent checkpoint, effectively reallocating agentic cognitive resources by implicitly sharpening their attention on the checkpoints. We further enhance the agent's checkpoint capabilities using a Multi-episode Agentic Reinforced Policy Optimization algorithm. On several long-horizon task benchmarks, CORAL significantly outperforms standard LLM agent methods. Notably, analysis of the LLMs' attention distribution reveals that CORAL substantially optimizes agentic RL dynamics, which in turn ensures agents maintain a focused cognitive resource allocation, thereby continuously amplifying performance gains.

results.title: Plan and Act: Enabling Agents to Solve Long Horizon Tasks
results.content: Recent work have found success by separating high-level planning from low-level execution, which leads to better alignment between high-level goals and environment-specific actions. However, planning remains inherently difficult for tasks like web navigation, often due to complex environmental dynamics and insufficient training data. To address these issues, Amir Gholami -- Research Scientist, UC Berkeley -- proposes Plan-and-Act, a novel framework that incorporates explicit planning into LLM-based agents. Plan-and-Act consists of a Planner model that generates high-level plans to achieve user goals and an Executor model that translates these plans into precise, environment-specific actions. By explicitly separating planning from execution, Plan-and- Act improves alignment between the [...] there's a lot of error correction that happens every And so we presented this uh last year at ICML called LLM compiler where the idea is that when when you have a user query you translate that user query into an intermediate representation and then you feed that intermediate representation to an LLM. So here is an example. Uh the user query is to click create a calendar invite with Luty and Sid at 2 p.m. tomorrow. So you have to first you know as an LLM you have to first go and figure out who is Luffy and Sid. So you have to make a query to the user's contact and maybe even dduplicate maybe there's multiple lries maybe there's multiple SIDs in their in their contact and once you have the email address then obviously you can go and create the calendar invite right so instead of feeding [...] an agent a teacher agent basically go and perform different actions on the web. Finally we have an OM model that looks at the action trajectory and decides which one of them is accurate. And once we have that then we pass this through another teacher LLM that creates plans that intermediate representation and then we again verify whether this plan is correct or not similar in spirit to what DeepCar1 did and then we basically can repeat this process and if you repeat this enough you can basically create enough set of data that you can fine-tune and create a very good um very good planning or reasoning data set and this approach is actually very very helpful. So we are currently with this approach able to beat the state-of-the-art with open source models on several web agent uh uh databases
